_comment: One file per procedure_code. Distilled from the matching pair in PM JAY/Standard Treatment Guideline/master/<condition>/ : _Medco.pdf (clinical + mandatory docs) + _PPD.CPD.pdf (PPD/CPD processing checklists). Filename = procedure_code. This is what the Documentation Checker, Query Predictor, and Medical Justification agents read AFTER the picker has chosen the code.
clinical_indications: Presence of thrombus within an artery in upper or lower extremities
Symptoms suggestive of acute limb ischemia (Pallor, Pain, Paresthesia, Paralysis, Pulselessness, Poikilothermia)

preauth.label: Clinical notes detailing history and admission notes showing vitals and examination findings.
Investigation reports such as Blood tests & Doppler Ultrasound

claim.label: Relevant Investigations report Blood tests & Doppler Ultrasound

ppd_preauth.q: Clinical notes detailing history and admission notes showing vitals and examination findings.
Were the investigation reports (Blood test & Doppler Ultrasound) submitted?
Were the relevant investigations MRI/CT/MRA submitted? (optional)
Were the patient's investigations (Blood Test & Doppler Ultrasound) suggestive of the diagnosis?

cpd_claim.q: Are the detailed Indoor case papers (ICPs) submitted?
Are the relevant investigation reports (Blood test & Doppler Ultrasound) submitted?
Is a detailed discharge summary submitted?

common_queries: Patient's investigations (Blood Test & Doppler Ultrasound) are not suggestive of Peripheral Arterial Thrombosis diagnosis or not documented.
Clinical notes detailing history and admission notes showing vitals and examination findings are missing or incomplete at pre-authorization.
Mandatory investigation reports (Blood tests & Doppler Ultrasound) are missing or incomplete at pre-authorization.
Detailed Indoor case papers (ICPs) or treatment details are missing or incomplete at claim submission.
Detailed discharge summary is missing or incomplete at claim submission.

clinical_key_pointers: Peripheral Arterial Thrombosis is the formation of a thrombus within an artery in the upper or lower extremities.
In most cases, it follows rupture of atheroma (a fat-rich deposit in the blood vessel wall), and is therefore referred to as atherothrombosis.
An arterial thrombus in the limbs can lead to acute limb ischemia.

Medical management is multifaceted and includes: Cholesterol reduction, Antiplatelet therapy, Anticoagulation, Peripheral vasodilators, Blood pressure management, Exercise therapy, and Smoking cessation.
Adherence to this regimen can reduce limb-related complications like critical limb ischemia and amputation, as well as systemic complications of atherosclerosis like stroke and myocardial infarction.
